subject: chemistry
educational_personality: exceptional classroom teacher; cream textbook; diagram teaches

lesson.big_idea: Acids and bases have different properties. Indicators help us classify them safely.

sections.title: Have You Wondered?
sections.body: Why does lemon juice taste sharp while soap feels slippery? Acids and bases behave differently.

sections.title: Using the Diagram
sections.body: See Acid → Indicator → Base. The diagram shows how an indicator reports what you have.

sections.title: Concept: Acids and Bases
sections.body: Acids often taste sour and can turn blue litmus red. Bases often feel soapy and can turn red litmus blue.

sections.title: Say It Simply
sections.body: An indicator is like a traffic light for chemistry — its colour tells you acid or base.

sections.title: In Real Life
sections.body: Vinegar on chips is acidic; baking soda used in cleaning is a base.

sections.title: Worked Example
sections.body: Dip litmus in lemon water: blue litmus turns red, so the sample behaves as an acid.

sections.title: Try This
sections.body: Point to Indicator and explain what a colour change tells you.

sections.title: Lesson Summary
sections.body: Acids and bases have different properties. Indicators help us identify them without tasting unknown chemicals.

sections.title: Think About It
sections.body: Why is tasting laboratory chemicals never acceptable, even if lemons are safe at home?

sections.title: Apply Your Learning
sections.body: Plan a safe indicator test using kitchen samples a teacher approves.

diagram_package.title: Acids and Bases
diagram_package.caption: Acid → Indicator → Base
diagram_package.explanation: This diagram teaches how the ideas in Acids and Bases connect.
diagram_package.practice_question: Point to Acid and give one safe everyday example.
